Coursera is great, but most of the courses I have done there were built in such way that if you do not achieve 100% score, you probably can not read. Exception being e.g. Pavel Pevzner's Bioinformatics specialisation or the original 2012 version of the Probabilistic Graphical Models course (has been diluted and split into several courses now, so not sure about that one any more).\n\nIn this respect, I found MITx courses on edX  better (and I like edX UX significantly more as well - yes, this is subjective). In order to pass those, you have to understand the subject matter, apply it in a creative way to a problem you have never seen before - so you have to actually think as opposed to Coursera's norm of simple recall quizzes. The only problem of those MITx courses is that they run on extremely crazy schedules, so having full-time job and/or family makes it almost impossible to do them simply because of lack of time.

The course difficulty really does vary by course. So far, they've been easy to moderately difficult, nothing too crazy. I'd look into some of the professional certificates, as the level of rigor may be higher there. \n\nedX is another great platform though - they usually have little to no overlap in university partnerships and courses (last I checked), so they're really good complements to one another.

As a person with no degree but interested in cyber security, would completing cyber security related certificates/courses allow me to step into the job field on a mid-high skill level?

If you have no degree or experience in cyber security, I would not set your expectations to jump into a mid-high level role. You'd be better suited to exploring entry level roles after you earn some certs. A degree would be even better, and there are some options through UC Berkeley and Syracuse University.

Can one put these on a resume and get a job for example Comptia + and Security + certs and get a job are they accepted by most employers or are these "decorative wallpaper" like a continuation learning which is nice and all lets employers know you seek improvement.

Hi! Yes, certainly worth including on your resume where appropriate (based on jobs you're applying for) and would be a great complement to some of the industry-recognized CompTIA certs. I've included coursework on my resume when it related to the role I applied for.

Hi! Unfortunately, no. 1. Free courses mean that all the content & assignments are available, whereas "non-free" courses give you the option to "audit";  this usually allows you to access alot of the content in a course, but not all of it until you pay a fee to pursue a certificate. 2. Do employers really recognize this as a true asset or skill??\n\n I'm really just wanting to upgrade my skills so I can get a better job.

As with any job hunt, your goal, in effect, is to provide evidence to prospective hiring managers that you're a good fit for a certain role. 

Having a verifiable certificate (what you could earn through Coursera, or other platforms) demonstrates you had dedicated time to learning a particular subject and have at least some baseline of knowledge. 

This, combined with relevant work experience, degrees, other certificates, adds to that evidence that a hiring manager looks for to be more confident in you as a prospective hire.

What do you think about MasterTrack certificate? i was thinking to do, but the price is high and i didnt find feedback

Hi - I think those are great options. Price point is a bit higher, but that's partly due to the fact that the courses you take can count towards transferrable credit to the corresponding full-degree program. ASU's Cybersecurity MasterTrack cert, for example, will get you 9 of the 30 required credits to actually pursue and complete the full degree.\n\nSo MasterTracks are, in a sense, a paid trial of a full degree where you can still earn a Certificate by completing the MasterTrack, but then you have the option of pursuing the full degree and be able to transfer credits and get a head start.
